worth_it_explanation: A programmatic deal is committed demand a specific buyer negotiated for, so a deal that does not deliver, or one that prices the inventory below its worth, is a direct revenue loss that no error message announces. The checks read from screens already in the account, the deal status, the line item priority, and the deal floor against open-auction clearing prices, and none of them needs new data or a rebuild. Reactivating a stalled deal, lifting a guaranteed deal to its proper priority, or raising a floor that fell behind the open market each adds revenue from the day it ships.
intro: Programmatic deals connect a specific buyer to a publisher's inventory at agreed terms, through Private Auctions, Preferred Deals, and Programmatic Guaranteed. Each is a negotiated commitment rather than open-market demand, so the inventory is meant to deliver to that buyer at the price both sides agreed to. These checks confirm that live deals are actually delivering, that guaranteed deals sit at a priority that protects the commitment, and that deal floors still reflect what the inventory is worth in open competition.

audit_checks.title: Programmatic deals are set up but not delivering
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal can be live in Google Ad Manager yet deliver nothing, which leaves committed demand unspent and the inventory monetizing at a lower rate than the negotiated deal would have paid. Because the deal still shows as active, the shortfall does not raise an error and the lost revenue goes unnoticed until someone reads the delivery.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal in Google Ad Manager (GAM) connects a named buyer to a publisher's inventory at terms both sides negotiated, whether as a Private Auction, a Preferred Deal, or a Programmatic Guaranteed campaign. Each deal carries a deal identifier (deal ID) that the buyer activates on their side, in a demand-side platform (DSP), so their bids can find the inventory the deal points to. A deal that is live on the publisher side is only half of the connection. The buyer side has to be active too, and the targeting on both sides has to describe the same inventory.

A deal that shows as active can still deliver nothing. The deal ID may never have been activated on the buyer side, so no bids ever arrive against it. The deal's targeting may not match available inventory, so the ad requests the buyer wants never reach the deal. Or a higher-priority line item may win the impressions the deal was meant to serve, so the buyer's bid is correct but loses every time. In each case the deal looks set up while the demand it represents never spends.

GAM's delivery reporting and its Private Auction troubleshooting view break down where a deal's opportunities went, separating a buyer that sent no bid response from bids that lost to competing demand or were filtered by a protection. That breakdown is what tells a stalled deal apart from a deal that is competing and losing. A deal with zero matched ad requests points to a targeting or activation gap, while a deal that bids but rarely wins points to a competing line item taking the impressions first.

A few signs point to a deal that is set up but not delivering:
  • A deal shows as active yet reports few or no impressions over a period when its inventory was serving
  • The buyer has not activated the deal ID on their side, so no bids arrive against it
  • The deal's targeting does not match the inventory that is actually receiving ad requests
  • A higher-priority line item is winning the impressions the deal was meant to serve
You may want to confirm the deal's status, the buyer's acceptance and activation, and the targeting match together before deciding a deal is healthy. A deal can be active on the publisher side and still deliver nothing if any one of those is missing.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, a deal is often negotiated around a section or audience that later gets restructured in the ad unit tree, so the deal's targeting no longer matches the inventory that serves. You may want to confirm each active deal's targeting still resolves to inventory that is receiving ad requests, rather than a section that was renamed or moved.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals are frequently scoped to seasonal or category inventory that only serves part of the year, so a deal can read as active while its inventory is dark. You may want to confirm a non-delivering deal is pointed at inventory that is currently live before treating the silence as a setup problem.

investigate.intro: Review each active deal's status, confirm the buyer has accepted and activated the deal on their side, and check that the deal's targeting matches inventory that is receiving ad requests.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Sales', then 'Programmatic', and review the list of deals. Note each deal's status and whether it shows as accepted by the buyer.
Open a deal that should be delivering and read its delivery, comparing impressions against the period its inventory was serving. Record any deal that shows few or no impressions while active.
Check the deal's targeting against the ad units and audiences that are receiving ad requests, and confirm the targeted inventory is currently live rather than renamed, moved, or seasonal.

For a Private Auction or Preferred Deal that is not delivering, open the Private Auction troubleshooting view and read the breakdown of where the opportunities went, separating a buyer that sent no bid response from bids that lost to competing demand.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Open 'Reporting' and build a report broken out by deal or deal ID, with impressions and revenue alongside the matched ad requests for the deal.
Identify deals that are active but report little or no delivery, and record each one with whether the gap looks like no bids arriving or bids arriving and losing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active deal, whether it is delivering, whether the buyer has accepted and activated it, and whether its targeting matches live inventory. A deal that is delivering, or one whose block to delivery is identified, for example a buyer that has not activated the deal ID or a higher-priority line item winning the impressions, passes once the cause is recorded. A deal that is active, reports little or no delivery, and has no identified cause is a fail recorded with the deal name so the gap can be traced.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal's delivery, buyer acceptance, and targeting match above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s programmatic deals are set up but not delivering. A deal that shows as active in Google Ad Manager can still spend nothing when the buyer never activated the deal identifier (deal ID) on their side, the targeting does not match available inventory, or a higher-priority line item wins the impressions, so committed demand goes unspent and the inventory monetizes at a lower rate than the deal would have paid. {{voice_recommend}} confirming each affected deal's buyer acceptance and activation, checking that its targeting resolves to inventory that is receiving ad requests, and reading the Private Auction troubleshooting view to separate a deal that gets no bids from one that bids and loses to competing demand. After the change, watch the deal's delivery in reporting to confirm it is spending.

audit_checks.title: Programmatic Guaranteed deals sit at the wrong delivery priority
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Programmatic Guaranteed is a committed deal that should deliver at a guaranteed priority. Left at a lower priority, it loses impressions to other demand and underdelivers, which risks the commitment to the buyer and can mean make-goods or a damaged relationship on top of the revenue that did not land.

educate.base:
Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) is a fixed-price programmatic deal where a publisher reserves a set amount of inventory for a single buyer at a negotiated price, the same commitment a direct sponsorship makes, transacted through a deal rather than a traditional insertion order. Because the publisher has promised the impressions, a PG deal is meant to deliver with the same protection a guaranteed direct campaign gets, ahead of non-guaranteed demand that competes for whatever is left.

In Google Ad Manager (GAM), delivery order follows the line item type and priority. Sponsorship and Standard line items, the guaranteed types, serve ahead of non-guaranteed types such as Preferred Deal, Private Auction, and the Open Auction. A PG deal creates a guaranteed line item, and when that line item carries the type and priority its guarantee calls for, it delivers against the commitment. When the line item is set as a lower-priority or non-guaranteed type, it competes for leftover impressions instead of being protected, and it can lose them to higher-priority demand even though the publisher committed to deliver.

The result is a PG deal that underdelivers against a promise. The buyer expected the reserved impressions, the publisher booked the revenue against them, and the misaligned priority costs both without raising an error. Underdelivery on a guarantee can trigger make-goods, where the publisher owes the shortfall in later inventory, so the cost is not only the missed revenue but the inventory spent making it right.

You may want to confirm that each Programmatic Guaranteed deal's line item carries a guaranteed type and a priority consistent with the commitment, rather than sitting among the non-guaranteed line items where it has to win its impressions in competition. The deal type at negotiation and the line item type that serves it should describe the same guarantee.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, a Programmatic Guaranteed deal is often scoped to high-value placements such as a homepage or a section takeover, the same inventory direct sponsorships want. You may want to confirm the deal's line item carries a guaranteed priority so it is protected against the direct and non-guaranteed demand competing for those same placements.

investigate.intro: Review the line items created by Programmatic Guaranteed deals and confirm each carries a guaranteed type and a priority consistent with the commitment it represents.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Sales', then 'Programmatic', and identify the deals transacted as Programmatic Guaranteed.

For each Programmatic Guaranteed deal, open the line item it created and note its line item type and priority. Confirm it is a guaranteed type, such as Sponsorship or Standard, rather than a non-guaranteed type such as Preferred Deal or Network.

Compare the line item's priority against the guaranteed direct campaigns on the same or similar inventory, and record any Programmatic Guaranteed deal whose line item sits at a lower priority than its commitment calls for.
Open the deal's delivery and check its delivery against the impressions it committed to, flagging any that are pacing behind the guarantee.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Open 'Reporting' and build a report broken out by deal or line item for the Programmatic Guaranteed deals, comparing delivered impressions against the goal.
Record any Programmatic Guaranteed deal that is underdelivering, so its priority and line item type can be checked against the commitment.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Programmatic Guaranteed deal, the line item type and priority that serves it and whether it is pacing to its committed impressions. A deal whose line item carries a guaranteed type and a priority that protects the commitment, and that is pacing to goal, passes. A deal whose line item sits at a non-guaranteed type or a priority below what the guarantee needs, or that is underdelivering for that reason, is a fail recorded with the deal name and the priority at issue.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Programmatic Guaranteed deal's line item type, priority, and delivery against its commitment above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals sit at a delivery priority below the commitment they represent. A PG deal reserves inventory for a buyer at a negotiated price, so it should deliver with the protection a guaranteed direct campaign gets, but a line item set as a non-guaranteed or lower-priority type competes for leftover impressions and loses them to higher-priority demand, which underdelivers against the promise and can trigger make-goods. {{voice_recommend}} aligning each affected deal's line item with a guaranteed type and a priority consistent with its guarantee, so it is protected rather than competing for what is left. After the change, watch the deal's pacing in reporting to confirm it is delivering to its committed impressions.

audit_checks.title: Preferred Deal and Private Auction floors sit below open-market clearing prices
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor that was set once and never revisited can fall below what the open auction already clears, so a favored buyer gets a discount on inventory that would have earned more in open competition. The loss is the difference between the deal price and the open-market price on every impression the deal wins, and nothing in the account flags it because the deal is delivering as configured.

educate.base:
A Preferred Deal and a Private Auction both carry a price the buyer transacts against. A Preferred Deal sets a fixed cost per thousand impressions (CPM) the buyer pays when it chooses to buy, and a Private Auction sets a floor, the minimum CPM a buyer has to bid to be eligible. Both prices are negotiated at a point in time and reflect what the inventory was worth then. Neither updates on its own as the open market moves.

The open auction puts a current price on the same inventory every day. When a deal's fixed price or floor was set high enough at the time, it gives the favored buyer a first or preferred opportunity at a fair rate. When that price was set once and never revisited, the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory can rise past it. The deal then hands a buyer inventory below what open competition was already paying, so the publisher earns the deal price on impressions that would have cleared higher in the open auction.

This loss is quiet because the deal is working exactly as configured. It delivers, the buyer pays the agreed price, and nothing reports an error. The only way to see it is to compare each deal's price or floor against the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory over a recent period. A deal floor sitting below the open-market clearing price is the signal that the negotiated price has fallen behind what the inventory is worth.

You may want to compare each active Preferred Deal price and Private Auction floor against the open-auction clearing price for the same ad units, and consider raising any floor that has drifted below the open market. A deal is meant to give a buyer a preferred rate, not a discount on inventory the open auction values more highly.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, audience or seasonal demand can lift the open-auction clearing price on a section well past a deal floor that was set in a quieter period. You may want to compare deal floors against the open-market price by section and time, so a floor set in a low season is not still discounting inventory that the open auction now values more.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, demand around shopping seasons can raise open-auction clearing prices sharply on the same inventory a deal is scoped to. You may want to confirm Preferred Deal prices and Private Auction floors on that inventory keep pace with the open market through peak periods rather than holding an off-season rate.

investigate.intro: Compare each active Preferred Deal price and Private Auction floor against the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory over a recent period.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Sales', then 'Programmatic', and list the active Preferred Deals and Private Auctions, recording each deal's fixed price or floor and the inventory it targets.
Note for each deal which ad units or sections it applies to, so its price can be matched to the open-auction performance on the same inventory.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Open 'Reporting' and build a report broken out by ad unit with an Open Auction or Ad Exchange average CPM metric for the inventory the deals target, over a recent period.
For each deal, compare its fixed price or floor against the open-auction average CPM on the same inventory, and record any deal whose price or floor sits below what the open market clears.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active Preferred Deal and Private Auction, its price or floor and the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory over a recent period. A deal whose price or floor sits at or above what the open market clears passes. A deal whose floor sits below the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory is a fail recorded with the deal name and the gap, so the floor can be raised to keep pace with the open market.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Preferred Deal price and Private Auction floor and the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s Preferred Deal prices and Private Auction floors sit below the open-auction clearing price for the same inventory. A deal floor set once and never revisited can fall behind the open market, so a favored buyer transacts at a rate the open auction has already passed, and the inventory earns the deal price on impressions that would have cleared higher in open competition. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each active deal's price or floor against the open-auction clearing price for the same ad units and raising any floor that has drifted below the open market. After the change, watch the affected inventory to confirm the deal price now sits at or above what the open auction clears.
